summary: Well-structured WalletConnect v2 integration with clean separation of concerns. Data models, serialization, URI parsing, and session management are all properly isolated. Tests are thorough with 66 test cases covering all TDD anchors. Minor suggestions below.

findings.finding: KDoc references [WalletConnectSignClient] which does not exist in this module. The comment mentions delegation to an interface that is not yet implemented.
findings.recommendation: Update the comment to reflect the current architecture, or remove the reference to the non-existent interface.

findings.finding: processQrCode calls validateRelayUrl again after parse() already validates relay URLs. Double validation is harmless but redundant.
findings.recommendation: Remove the redundant relay validation in processQrCode since parse() already handles it.

findings.finding: WalletConnectError uses class-based sealed children instead of data class or object. InvalidQrUri and RelayConnectionFailed create new instances on every occurrence.
findings.recommendation: Consider using 'data class' for error types that carry data, and 'object' for those that don't (InvalidQrUri, RelayConnectionFailed, SessionExpired). However, this is purely stylistic for a sealed class hierarchy.

findings.finding: testOptions.unitTests.isReturnDefaultValues = true enables returning default values for unmocked Android framework methods. This is generally fine for this module since it uses java.net.URI instead of android.net.Uri.
findings.recommendation: Keep as-is. The option provides safety net for any Android framework classes that might be transitively referenced.

passed_checks: Session lifecycle (approve, reject, remove) works correctly
24h expiry logic is sound with >= comparison at boundary
Relay validation covers both allowed domains and rejects subdomains
URI parser handles all edge cases (empty, v1, missing params)
SessionSerializer round-trips all fields including null icon and empty arrays
TimeProvider abstraction enables deterministic time testing
StateFlow pattern properly exposes immutable state to UI
No Android framework dependencies in testable code (uses java.net.URI)
SecureStorage properly mocked in tests without needing internal access
